1.synthesis: In the sharpest possible contrast to ch. 34's desolation-of-Edom, ch. 35 opens with a vision of universal ecological restoration: the wilderness and the dry land will be glad; the desert will rejoice and blossom like the crocus (v.1). It will blossom abundantly, and shout for joy and sing! — the glory of Lebanon, the majesty of Carmel and Sharon, the glory of YHWH and the majesty of our God (v.2). Into this ecological jubilation, the prophet addresses weak hands and feeble knees (v.3): “Strengthen the weak hands and steady the feeble knees; say to those with anxious heart: Be strong! Do not fear! Your God will come with vengeance, with the recompense of God; he himself will come and save you” (v.4).
1.historical_context: Ch. 35 forms the second half of the 34–35 diptych: judgment (34) and salvation (35). The specific landscapes invoked — Lebanon's cedar glory, Carmel's vineyards, Sharon's coastal plain — are the same regions that mourned in Isaiah 33:9; their rejoicing here constitutes a precise reversal. The crocus (ḥăbaṣṣelet) was associated with the spring blooming of the Sharon plain — a particularly vivid image of explosive fertility after the dry winter. The address to “weak hands and feeble knees” (v.3) picks up the conventional posture of soldiers or civilians in terror (Ezekiel 7:17; Hebrews 12:12) and promises that divine vengeance and salvation are the same coming event.
1.literary_structure: The opening verses alternate between the natural world rejoicing (vv.1–2a) and the divine source of that joy (v.2b: the glory of YHWH). The pastoral address of vv.3–4 interrupts the landscape vision to bring its message to the anxious human community: the ecological restoration is inseparable from the salvation of the fearful. The coming of God is simultaneously vengeance against the enemy (Isaiah 34:8) and salvation for the weak. The crocus-blooming and the steadied knee belong to the same divine advent.
1.theological_application: The promise that YHWH “himself will come and save you” (v.4) is one of the most direct divine-advent promises in the Hebrew prophets. The ecological restoration of the desert is not merely environmental but sacramental: the transformation of the wilderness landscape is the outward sign of YHWH's arrival to save his people. The address to the anxious-hearted places this promise squarely in the arena of pastoral care: the physical weakness of trembling knees corresponds to spiritual fear, and both are addressed by the same divine coming.
1.christology: When John the Baptist sends from prison to ask whether Jesus is the coming one, Jesus answers with the signs of ch. 35: “the blind see, the lame walk, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news preached to them” (Matthew 11:5), directly quoting vv.5–6. Jesus' healing miracles are not merely compassionate acts but prophetic signs declaring that YHWH himself has come. The crocus blooming in the wilderness is the first flower of the new creation inaugurated in Christ's resurrection, whose Spirit turns every spiritual desert into a garden.

5.synthesis: The human physical healings cascade in vv.5–6: blind eyes opened, deaf ears unstopped, the lame leaping like a deer, the mute tongue singing for joy. Simultaneously, the wilderness waters break forth: “waters shall break forth in the wilderness, and streams in the desert; the burning sand shall become a pool, and the thirsty ground springs of water” (vv.6–7). The parallelism is deliberate: the healing of the human body and the healing of the landscape are dimensions of the same divine renewal. The “lair of jackals” (the desolate animal haunt of ch. 34) becomes “grass, reeds, and rushes” — habitat for life rather than death.
5.historical_context: The fourfold human healing — blind, deaf, lame, mute — reverses the fourfold human incapacity of the judicial blindness passages (Isaiah 6:9–10; Isaiah 29:9–12). The wilderness/desert motif (v.6b–7) connects to the new exodus: YHWH will again provide water in the wilderness as he did in the Sinai journey (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11). The burning sand (šārāb) in v.7 is the shimmering mirage of the desert — false water that cannot satisfy — replaced by real pools and springs. The jackals' lair (v.7b) connects backward to the animal habitation of ruined Edom (ch. 34) to complete the ecological reversal.
5.literary_structure: The section arranges four human healings in two parallel pairs — blind/deaf (v.5) and lame/mute (v.6a) — before opening into two landscape transformations (v.6b–7). The pairing of human and ecological healing is a feature of Isaianic eschatology: the new creation is simultaneously biological (human bodies) and environmental (land and water). The deer image (kāʾayyāl, v.6) is joyfully kinetic — not just standing upright but leaping; not just speaking but singing. The range of transformation moves from interior (blindness, deafness) through physical action (leaping, singing) to landscape (waters, pools, springs).
5.theological_application: The promise of physical healing in the age of salvation raises questions about the relationship between present healing ministry and eschatological fulfilment. Jesus' healings are sign-events, firstfruits of the full harvest. The church lives between the already-inaugurated healing of the kingdom (manifest in Christ's ministry and in the Spirit's gifts) and the not-yet-complete physical restoration of the new creation. The parallel healing of human bodies and damaged landscapes anticipates the Christian vision of a new creation where death, disease, and ecological degradation are all simultaneously reversed.
5.christology: Jesus' literal healings of the blind, deaf, lame, and mute were not merely humanitarian but eschatological declarations: they were the credentials of the coming one (Matthew 11:5), signs that YHWH himself had come to save. The leaping of the lame and the singing of the mute (v.6) find their paradigmatic fulfilment in the lame man who leaps and praises God at the Beautiful Gate in Acts 3:8, invoking the name of Jesus. The living water that springs up in the desert (v.7) is explicitly fulfilled in Jesus' offer of water springing up to eternal life (John 4:14; John 7:38).

8.synthesis: The chapter closes with its most celebrated image: “And a highway shall be there, and it shall be called the Way of Holiness (derekh haqqōdeš)” (v.8). The unclean will not travel on it; it will belong to those who walk in it, even fools who are redeemed will not go astray on it. No lion, no ravenous beast — none will be there. The redeemed shall walk there, and the ransomed of YHWH shall return and come to Zion with singing, with everlasting joy upon their heads; sorrow and sighing shall flee away (v.10). This is the new exodus road — the homeward journey of the ransomed community.
8.historical_context: The “Way of Holiness” image picks up the ancient Near Eastern concept of the sacred processional road — the great roads in Babylon and Egypt along which deities were carried in procession. But Isaiah subverts the genre: this highway is not for the transport of a divine image but for the return of ransomed human beings to the presence of their God. The “redeemed” (gĕʾûlîm) and “ransomed” (pĕdûyê YHWH) language draws on the exodus tradition: YHWH the kinsman-redeemer (gōʾēl) buys back his enslaved people at great cost. The fool who will not go astray on this road (v.8) suggests that even those of limited spiritual capacity will find their way home when YHWH makes the path clear.
8.literary_structure: The highway oracle (v.8) transitions from the ecological restoration (vv.1–7) to the human homecoming (vv.9–10), providing the road on which the healed will travel. The two final elements — no ravenous beasts (v.9) and the ransomed returning with joy (v.10) — answer the two threats of the journey: external danger (predators on the road) and internal despair (sorrow and sighing). The everlasting joy (śimḥat ʿôlām) crowning the returners' heads replaces the mourning garments they wore in exile. The phrase “sorrow and sighing shall flee away” (v.10) is repeated verbatim in Isaiah 51:11, making this image a refrain of the whole Book of Comfort.
8.theological_application: The Way of Holiness is both a specific new-exodus road and a theological vision of the path to redemption: it is holy (belonging to the holy God), exclusive (the unclean cannot travel it), safe (no predators), and well-marked (even fools will not err in it). The combination of holiness and accessibility — requiring moral correspondence yet being navigable even by the spiritually simple — speaks to both the demand and the grace of the gospel. The ransomed community travels homeward not by their own navigation but by the light of the divine road-builder who has cleared the way.
8.christology: Jesus' self-declaration “I am the way” (John 14:6) draws on precisely this Isaianic tradition of the holy highway. He is the derekh haqqōdeš made personal — the road to the Father, the route of the ransomed, the way that even the simplest disciple can follow because the Son has pioneered it. Hebrews describes the new and living way opened through the curtain of his flesh (Hebrews 10:20), and the entire pilgrim-community of faith travels toward the city whose maker and builder is God. The everlasting joy of v.10 is the eschatological state of those who arrive — the joy of the new Jerusalem, the marriage supper of the Lamb.
